description: BitBio Pixel Art Studio master knowledge base. Compiled from analysis of 20+ landmark pixel art games. Used directly by tooling agents to validate and generate art assets.

keyPrinciples: strong silhouette over detail
near-black voids create depth
2-3 color tiles maximum
functional > decorative
emotion via tiny faces
more detailed than Undertale but same philosophy
zone identity through palette
thick black outlines
walk animation = 4 frames
consistent head-to-body ratio 1:1.3
thick 4px outlines define everything
grayscale white world vs vibrant headspace
emotion conveyed by eye shape alone
rooms are minimalist — negative space is design
color saturation signals psychological state
16x16 tiles create rooms
overworld sprites taller than earlier gens
indoor/outdoor palette separation
NPC variety through recolors
clean diagonal shading on sprites
era-specific palettes create time period identity
limited SNES palette used brilliantly
2.5D perspective on overworld
boss battles use full-screen sprites
consistent color ramps: 3 values per hue
PSI effects: rainbow color cycling
enemy sprites = everyday objects distorted
walking animation = 2 frames only
deliberately crude art = charm
backgrounds scroll at different speeds to fake parallax
warm earthy palette conveys comfort
16x16 tiles but characters taller
seasonal palette shifts (same world, different mood)
day/night = hue shift + darkening
variety through 8 character frames per direction
tiny 8px character reads at full clarity due to silhouette
each biome = 2 accent colors max on dark bg
hair color = player state indicator
trail effects communicate movement feeling
screen-shake + particle juice over detailed sprites
neon-on-dark: saturated colors POP against near-black
glow effects via transparent colored squares
particle density creates richness
limited animation frames but very readable
each zone = 1 dominant hue
richest pixel art palette in modern games (500+ colors used)
every tile has 5+ shades
character sprites have 12+ colors each
ambient occlusion faked via dark corners on every tile
lighting changes hue not just brightness
pseudo-3D isometric with pixel art sprites
extremely tight color discipline across hundreds of assets
enemies communicate danger level via color temperature
terrain reading: warm = safe, cool = dangerous
consistent 3/4 view perspective
modern SNES homage with more colors
time of day via palette cycling (same tiles, different hue/brightness)
extremely detailed tile work (60+ fillRect equivalent per tile)
hero sprites have consistent palette across all outfits
boss sprites fill entire battle screen
mode 7 + sprites creates dramatic scale
characters identifiable in 16x24
color coding: Terra=teal, Locke=red, Shadow=dark
steampunk industrial palette (browns+grays with one bright color)
menu system uses full SNES color depth
GBA palette constraints used creatively (32 colors per sprite)
djinn sprites are instantly iconic despite 16x16
town tiles are lush for GBA hardware
battle backgrounds use Mode 7 equivalent
elemental colors: Venus=brown, Mercury=blue, Mars=red, Jupiter=purple
faction identity via palette (Hector=blue, Eliwood=red, Lyn=green)
map sprites extremely readable at 16x16
portrait art style vs overworld style (deliberate contrast)
class identity through silhouette
terrain tiles communicate gameplay: forest=cover, water=no movement
large enemy sprites create threat scale
Gothic architecture conveyed with 3 dark tile shades
player sprite is recognizable despite GBA tiny screen
soul orbs use warm yellow against dark backgrounds
boss sprites often fill half the screen
atmosphere via color temperature (warm = safe, cool-blue = infected/dangerous)
Samus silhouette readable at any size
parasite infected areas: desaturated blue-gray
SA-X encounters: dread via familiar-but-wrong silhouette
rooms communicate threat before enemies appear

silhouetteTheory.rule: A sprite's outline shape alone must communicate character identity, class, and emotional state.
silhouetteTheory.why: Players read silhouettes before details — in fast gameplay, the shape IS the character.
silhouetteTheory.test: Blur the sprite to 1/4 resolution. Can you still tell who it is?
silhouetteTheory.howToBreak: Only when a character's confusion/disguise is the point (e.g. SA-X in Metroid Fusion).
silhouetteTheory.bitbioApplication: Player sprite needs distinct hair silhouette per customization. Each NPC needs unique head shape + distinguishing silhouette element.

paletteConstruction.rule: Build color ramps with hue shift: shadows shift cool, highlights shift warm. Never just darken/lighten.
paletteConstruction.why: Flat darkening looks muddy. Hue-shifted ramps feel alive and are easier to distinguish.
paletteConstruction.test: Convert sprite to grayscale — do the colors still feel intentional?
paletteConstruction.exampleGood: Undertale Ruins: shadow #4a2000 (red-orange) → highlight #d4a56a (shifts toward yellow)
paletteConstruction.bitbioApplication: All 4 realms need proper hue-shifted ramps, not just lightened versions of the base color.

colorCount.rule: Maximum 8 colors per sprite, 16 per tile set, 32 per scene.
colorCount.why: Fewer colors = more cohesion, easier to make harmonious, lower cognitive load.
exceptions: Eastward uses 500+ but has dedicated artist per asset
Sea of Stars uses 20+ per scene but strict zone discipline
colorCount.bitbioApplication: Each realm palette: 1 dark bg + 3 floor shades + 2 wall shades + 1 accent + 1 bright accent = 8 colors max

tileSeamlessness.rule: A tile must look correct when placed adjacent to itself in all 4 directions.
tileSeamlessness.why: Maps repeat tiles. Visible seams break immersion instantly.
tileSeamlessness.test: Place 9 copies in a 3x3 grid. Do seams disappear?
tileSeamlessness.technique: Edges must be 1px lighter or darker than 1px inside — not the same as the adjacent tiles' opposite edge.
tileSeamlessness.bitbioApplication: All floor tiles need seam-hiding edge treatment.

valueContrast.rule: Characters must have higher contrast against backgrounds than anything else on screen.
valueContrast.why: Player can't control what they can't see. Character readability is gameplay.
valueContrast.undertaleApproach: Dark backgrounds (#000000-#0a0a0a) + white/light character outlines = instant readability
valueContrast.bitbioApplication: All realms use near-black floors. Characters use lighter base colors. NPC outline colors must be darker than floor.

walkCycle.description: Neutral → forward-lean → neutral → back-lean. Or simpler: right-foot-forward → neutral → left-foot-forward → neutral.
walkCycle.undertaleApproach: 2-frame walk: legs alternate, whole body bobs slightly
walkCycle.stardewApproach: 4 frames: full arm swing + leg movement

idleAnimation.description: Subtle breathing or eye blink. 1-2 pixels of movement maximum.
idleAnimation.notes: Long idle loops feel more alive than short ones. Undertale NPCs have 4-8 frame idles.

anticipation.description: Brief backward motion before a forward action. 1-2 frames.
anticipation.importance: Makes actions feel weighty rather than instantaneous.

smearFrames.description: Intentionally blurred/stretched single frames during fast motion.

characterDesign.headBodyRatio: 1:1 to 1:1.5 for chibi/RPG styles. Larger heads = more expressive faces.
characterDesign.eyeDesign: Eyes communicate personality. Large round eyes = friendly/young. Narrow = serious. Abstract dots = uncanny/horror.
characterDesign.colorCoding: Use consistent color association: fire=red, nature=green, tech=cyan/blue, magic=purple.
characterDesign.silhouetteVariety: Each NPC should have a unique silhouette element: unusual hair, held object, body proportion difference.

environmentDesign.readability: Walkable areas should be visually distinct from walls. Floor = flat, Wall = raised/textured.
environmentDesign.depthLayers: Background (parallax) → Middleground (tiles) → Foreground (objects) → Character layer → UI
environmentDesign.lightingFake: Place colored semi-transparent tiles under light sources. No actual lighting calculation needed.
environmentDesign.atmosphericPerspective: Distant elements = more desaturated, slightly blue-shifted. Near = vivid.
environmentDesign.interestPoints: Never more than 16 tiles between visual interest points (decorative tiles, objects, NPCs).

colorTheory.hueShifting: As value decreases (shadows), shift hue toward cool (blue/purple). As value increases (highlights), shift toward warm (yellow/orange).
colorTheory.saturationManagement: Mid-tones have highest saturation. Shadows and highlights both desaturate slightly. Exception: neon-on-dark like Hyper Light Drifter.
colorTheory.contrastControl: 80% of pixel art mistakes are contrast problems. Check in grayscale. Your darkest dark should be <20 lightness, brightest bright >80 lightness.
colorTheory.colorTemperature: Warm (red/orange/yellow) = safe, inviting, alive. Cool (blue/purple/green) = dangerous, alien, dead. This is used universally across all games.

paletteHarmony.analogous: Colors next to each other on color wheel. Safe, cohesive. Used for zone palettes.
paletteHarmony.complementary: Opposite colors. High contrast. Use for accents only (e.g., teal accent on red-brown Ruins).
paletteHarmony.triad: Three equidistant colors. Used when you need multiple distinct elements to read separately.

bitbioSettings.helperFunction: gr(ctx, cx, cy, gx, gy, gw, gh, color)

cytoplasm.description: Ocean of life — teal/cyan on near-black

genomeForest.description: Ancient DNA wilderness — deep green on near-black

neuralNebula.description: Consciousness space — electric purple on void black

proteinCathedral.description: Ancient molecular temple — gold on near-black stone
